1: ben-šĕtêm ʿeśreh šānāh mĕnaššeh bĕmolkô — ‘Manasseh was twelve years old when he became king.’ Born during Hezekiah’s fifteen-year extension after his illness (32:24-26), Manasseh is the product of the reprieve. His fifty-five year reign (ḥămiššîm waḥāmēš šānāh) is the longest of any Judahite king, exceeding Solomon (forty years) and David (forty years). The length of the reign contrasts sharply with its theological content, creating the Chronicler’s most sustained portrait of apostasy-then-restoration.
2: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bĕʿênê YHWH — ‘He did evil in the eyes of YHWH.’ The standard apostasy formula. kĕtôʿăḇôt haggôyim = ‘according to the abominations of the nations.’ tôʿēḇāh (abomination) is the Deuteronomic term for practices that are fundamentally incompatible with YHWH’s holiness (Deut 7:25-26; 12:31; 18:9-12). The Chronicler specifies which nations YHWH drove out before Israel — their practices are precisely what Israel was prohibited from imitating.
3: wayyāšāḇ wayyiḇen ʾet-habbāmôt ʾăšer nittaṣ ḥizkîyāhû ʾāḇîw — ‘He rebuilt the high places that his father Hezekiah had demolished.’ The reversal verb wayyāšāḇ (returned/went back; Qal of šûḇ) applied to cult practice signals apostasy as retrogression. The father’s reform work (nittaṣ, Niphal of nāṭaš: was pulled down/demolished) is undone by the son. wayyiḵrōṯ mizbĕḥôt labbe bĕ ālîm = set up altars for the Baals; wayyaʿaś ʾăšērôt = made Asherah poles. The Baals and Asherah represent the primary Canaanite fertility cult complex.
4: wayyiḇen mizbĕḥôt bĕbêt YHWH — ‘He built altars in the house of YHWH.’ The desecration of the temple is the central apostasy: foreign altars installed in YHWH’s own house. The Chronicler notes the divine counterpoint: ʾăšer ʾāmar YHWH bîrûšālayim yihyeh šĕmî lĕʿôlām = ‘of which YHWH had said: In Jerusalem my name shall be forever.’ The ‘name-dwelling’ theology (Deuteronomy’s šēm formula) is invoked precisely here to show the magnitude of the desecration: the permanently named sanctuary is being contaminated.
5: mizbĕḥôt lĕkol-ṣĕḇāʾ haššāmayim — altars for ‘all the host of heaven.’ ṣĕḇāʾ haššāmayim (host of heaven) = the stellar/planetary deities of Mesopotamian astral religion. Altars to these were placed in both courts of the temple (bišĕtê ḥaṣrôt bêt-YHWH). Astral worship was associated with Assyrian influence (2 Kgs 21:3-5 parallels this) and represents the cultural-imperial pressure of Assyrian hegemony on Judahite cult. Deut 17:3 explicitly prohibits worship of ṣĕḇāʾ haššāmayim.
6: wĕhûʾ heʿbîr ʾet-bānāyw bāʾēš bĕgêʾ ben-hinnōm — ‘He passed his sons through fire in the valley of Ben Hinnom.’ Child sacrifice at the Hinnom valley (the historical site of later ‘Gehenna’) is repeated from ch. 28:3 (Ahaz). The catalog of occult practices that follows uses the full technical vocabulary of Deut 18:10-14: ʿônēn (one who observes times/divines, Piel of ʿānan), niḥēš (practiced divination by omens, Piel of nāḥaš), kēšep (used sorcery, from kaššāp), ʿāśāh ʾôḇ wĕyiddĕʿōnî (dealt with mediums and spiritists). Deuteronomy lists these as the defining practices of the nations driven out before Israel; Manasseh reinstates all of them.
7: wayyāśem ʾet-pesel hassĕmel ʾăšer ʿāśāh bĕbêt hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘He set the carved image of the idol that he made in the house of God.’ pesel (carved image) is the idol-vocabulary of the Second Commandment (Exod 20:4). hassĕmel = ‘the idol/likeness’ (a rare term, appearing also in Ezek 8:3, 5 for the detestable image in the temple vision). YHWH had said of this temple (ʾel-šĕlōmōh): ‘In this house and in Jerusalem... I will put my name forever.’ Installing a carved idol in the named sanctuary is the direct negation of everything the Solomonic temple represents.
8: wĕlōʾ ʾôsîp lĕhāšîm ʾet-regel yiśrāʾēl — ‘I will no more cause the foot of Israel to move.’ The conditional promise embedded in YHWH’s word about the temple: Israel’s stability (rāgal = foot/footing) depends on keeping the Torah (wĕšāmĕrû lāʿăśôt ʾēt kol-ʾăšer ṣiwwîtî). ʾal-mâh + the giving of the Torah = the conditional structure of the Davidic-Solomonic covenant. The negative formulation (‘I will not again move them’) makes the implicit threat explicit: violate these conditions and the foot will move — which is exactly what happens in exile.
9: wayyaṭʿeh mĕnaššeh ʾet-yĕhûḏāh wĕʾet-yôšĕḇê yĕrûšālayim — ‘Manasseh led Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem astray.’ tāʿāh (Hiphil: to cause to wander, lead astray) is the apostasy-leadership verb: what the king does, the nation does. lāʿăśôt rāʿ min-haggôyim = ‘to do more evil than the nations.’ The comparative min (more than) creates a superlative of apostasy: Manasseh exceeded the Canaanites whom Israel displaced. This is the strongest negative verdict in Chronicles; the parallel in 2 Kgs 21:9 also uses the comparative.
10: wayyḏabbēr YHWH ʾel-mĕnaššeh wĕʾel-ʿammô wĕlōʾ hiqqîšîbû — ‘YHWH spoke to Manasseh and his people, but they paid no attention.’ qāšaḇ (Hiphil: to give attention, listen carefully) in the negative: wĕlōʾ hiqqîšîbû = they did not attend. The image is of someone speaking clearly and directly while the hearer refuses to orient. The Chronicler does not specify the form of YHWH’s speech (prophet? Scripture?), leaving it as a general divine address that was deliberately ignored.
11: wayyāḇēʾ YHWH ʿălêhem ʾet-śārê ṣĕḇāʾ melek ʾaššûr — ‘YHWH brought against them the commanders of the army of the king of Assyria.’ The divine subject of Assyrian military action is the Chronicler’s key framing: the Assyrians are YHWH’s instrument, not autonomous aggressors. wayyilkĕḏû ʾet-mĕnaššeh baḥôḥîm = ‘they captured Manasseh with hooks.’ ḥôaḥ (hook, thorn) used for leading captives is confirmed by Assyrian reliefs showing captives led by hooks through their lips or nose. wayyeʾasrûhû bĕnaḥoštayim = bound him with bronze fetters (naḥôšet = bronze/copper; dual naḥuštayim = double-chains). wayyôlîḵûhû bāḇelāh = brought him to Babylon.
12: ûḵĕhāṣar lô ḥillāh ʾet-pĕnê YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw — ‘When he was in distress, he entreated the face of YHWH his God.’ ṣārar (to be in straits, distress) in the infinitive of temporal clause: when he was pressed/confined (ḵĕhāṣar lô). ḥālāh pānê YHWH = ‘he sought the face of YHWH’ (lit. ‘made smooth/sick the face’ — an idiom for imploring, from the image of prostrating in need). wayyiḵkānēʿ mĕʾōḏ = ‘he humbled himself greatly.’ The intensifier mĕʾōḏ (greatly, exceedingly) marks the depth of the repentance. kānaʿ appears here in one of its most dramatic OT applications: the worst king produces the most intense humbling.
13: wayyitpallēl ʾēlāyw wayyēʿātēr lô — ‘He prayed to him and he was entreated by him.’ ʿātar (Niphal: to be moved/entreated by prayer) is used of divine responsiveness to petition (Gen 25:21; Exod 8:30; 2 Sam 21:14). wayyišmaʿ tĕḥinnātô = ‘he heard his supplication.’ tĕḥinnāh (supplication, plea for grace) is the strongest petition term, from ḥānan (to be gracious, show favor). The restoration is politically concrete: wayyᵃšîḇēhû yĕrûšālayim lĕmalkûtô = ‘he restored him to Jerusalem to his kingdom.’ The Chronicler’s unique addition concludes: wayyēḏaʿ mĕnaššeh kî YHWH hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm = ‘then Manasseh knew that YHWH is God.’ The goal of the exile is yāḏaʿ (knowledge): the worst apostasy produces the clearest confession.
14: After his restoration Manasseh built a second outer wall for the city of David west of the Gihon spring, through the valley, to the entrance of the Fish Gate, encircling Ophel (ʾōpel). The topographic specificity (Gihon spring, Fish Gate, Ophel) corresponds to archaeological identifications in the City of David. wayyāgĕḇah mĕʾōḏ = the wall was made very high. Military commanders (śārê-ḥayil) were placed in all the fortified cities.
15: wayyāsar ʾet-ʾĕlōhê hannēkār — ‘He removed the foreign gods.’ Manasseh’s reform catalog: removing foreign gods, the idol from the temple, the altars he had built from the mountain of YHWH’s house, and casting them outside the city. wayyᵉkōnnēn ʾet-mizbaḥ YHWH = he restored the altar of YHWH (Piel of kûn: to establish, restore to its proper state). The reform is the structural reverse of the apostasy catalog in vv. 3-7.
16: wayyizbaḥ ʿālāyw zibḥê šĕlāmîm ûtôḏôt — ‘He sacrificed on it peace offerings and thank offerings.’ šelāmîm (peace/fellowship offerings) and tôḏôt (thank offerings, plural of tôḏāh) represent the voluntary response sacrifices of gratitude, not obligatory atonement sacrifices. Manasseh commands Judah to serve YHWH God of Israel. The reform reaches its social dimension: from personal conviction to national command. The Chronicler notes the incomplete result: the people continued sacrificing at high places, ‘only to YHWH their God’ (raq lĕ-YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem rāq).
17: wĕhaʿām ʿôḏ mĕzabbĕḥîm babbāmôt — ‘But the people still sacrificed at the high places.’ The failure of the reform to fully purge the regional shrines — even after Manasseh’s dramatic restoration — signals the depth of syncretism’s entrenchment. The Chronicler’s note raq lĕ-YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem (only to YHWH their God) distinguishes this from outright idolatry: it is unauthorized worship of the right God, not worship of wrong gods. But the Deuteronomic theology (Deut 12:4-14) makes the location of worship as important as the object.
18: wĕyeter diḇrê mĕnaššeh ûtĕpillātô — ‘The rest of the acts of Manasseh and his prayer.’ The Chronicler references ‘his prayer’ (tĕpillātô) as a source document, now recorded in the acts of the kings of Israel. An apocryphal text, The Prayer of Manasseh, survives in the Apocrypha and is associated with this reference, though whether it is the original prayer or a later composition inspired by this verse is disputed. The prophetic sources cited: the seers (ḥōzîm) who spoke to Manasseh in the name of YHWH God of Israel. ḥōzeh (seer) is the visionary prophetic title, distinct from the speaking prophet (nāḇîʾ).
19: ûtĕpillātô wayyēʿātēr lô wĕkol-ḥattāʾtô ûmaʿlô — ‘His prayer and how God was moved by him, and all his sin and his unfaithfulness.’ ḥattāʾt (sin, offense) and māʿal (treachery, covenant-unfaithfulness) are both cited; the comprehensive record of apostasy is noted alongside the prayer record. The Chronicler emphasizes the sites before he humbled himself (lipnê hikkānĕʿô) where altars were built — making the restoration forensically detailed.
20: wayyiškab mĕnaššeh ʿim-ʾăḇôtāyw — ‘Manasseh slept with his fathers.’ wayyiqbĕrûhû bĕḇêtô = buried in his house. The burial location (in his house rather than in the royal tombs) is unusual; 2 Kgs 21:18 adds ‘in the garden of his house, in the garden of Uzza.’ Whether burial in a private garden rather than the royal tomb reflects his compromised legacy or simply reflects changing burial customs is uncertain.
21: Amon was twenty-two years old when he became king (ben-ʿeśrîm ûštayim šānāh ʾāmôn bĕmolkô) and reigned two years in Jerusalem (ûštayim šānîm mālaḵ bîrûšālāyim). The brevity of Amon’s reign (the shortest in Judah alongside Amaziah’s assassination) signals its catastrophic trajectory even before the content is described. The standard evaluation follows: he did evil in YHWH’s eyes.
22: wayyizbah lĕkol-happĕsîlîm ʾăšer ʿāśāh ʾāmôn ʾāḇîw — ‘He sacrificed to all the carved images that his father Amon [sic — Manasseh] had made.’ The text may have a copying error (Amon is listed as both the king and his father); the intended referent is Manasseh. The point is: Amon restored the idols Manasseh had removed, but unlike Manasseh, Amon never humbled himself. wĕlōʾ hiḵkānēʿ mippe nê YHWH = ‘he did not humble himself before YHWH.’ The absence of kānaʿ is the decisive negative.
23: kî Amon hurbāh ʾāšam — ‘Amon greatly increased his guilt.’ The verb hirbāh (Hiphil of rāḇāh: to multiply, increase) applied to ʾāšam (guilt) describes the quantitative accumulation of unfaithfulness. This is the Chronicler’s familiar compounding-sin formula (cf. 28:22 on Ahaz). Unlike Manasseh, Amon takes the apostasy deeper without the corrective of exile or humbling.
24: wayyiqŝrû ʿālāyw ʿăḇādāyw wayyĕmîtûhû bĕḇêtô — ‘His servants conspired against him and killed him in his house.’ qāšar (to conspire, bind together) is the regicide-conspiracy verb (cf. 2 Chr 24:21 for Joash; 2 Kgs 9:14 for Jehu). The killing in his house indicates an inside plot by court officials. The motive is unspecified, but palace coups in the ancient Near East typically combined political grievances with opportunism.
25: wayyakkû ʿam-hāʾāreṣ ʾet kol-haqqōšĕrîm ʿal-ʾāmôn — ‘The people of the land struck down all who had conspired against Amon.’ The ʿam hāʾāreṣ (people of the land) is a recurring social actor in Chronicles and Kings, representing a conservative constituency that acts to restore dynastic legitimacy when it is threatened by coups. They execute the conspirators and install Josiah. wayyamlîḵû ʾet-yōʾšîyāhû ḇĕnô taḥtāyw = they made Josiah his son king in his place. The ʿam hāʾāreṣ’s role here parallels their action after Athaliah’s coup (2 Chr 23:13, 21): they are a check on palace illegitimacy.
